title: Characteristic Curves, Film Speed, and Film Selection
narrationScript: Every type of industrial film behaves differently, and the document that captures that behavior is the characteristic curve, also called the Hurter and Driffield curve, or H and D curve, after the two scientists who first plotted it. Learn to read it and you can predict what any film will do before you ever load a cassette. The curve plots density on the vertical axis against the logarithm of relative exposure on the horizontal. Why the logarithm? Because film responds to ratios, not raw amounts. The same curve shape applies whether exposures are short or long, and equal steps along the log axis mean equal multiplications of exposure. Three tenths of a log step is a factor of two. The curve tells its story in regions. It starts not at zero density but at base plus fog, the density of film that received no exposure at all, the tint of the base plus the few grains that develop anyway. Fresh film handled and processed correctly sits around two to three tenths. Then comes the toe, where density climbs slowly and small exposure differences barely register. Contrast is poor down there, which is exactly why codes set minimum density requirements. A radiograph living in the toe hides discontinuities. Above the toe the curve steepens into its working region, and here is the special character of industrial X-ray film. It keeps getting steeper as density rises through the entire useful range, and only at densities beyond what an ordinary illuminator can punch through does it finally roll over into a shoulder. The slope of the curve at any point is called the gradient, and gradient is film contrast. The steeper the curve, the bigger the density difference produced by a given difference in the radiation passing through the part, and the easier it is to see a small thickness change, like a flaw. Because gradient grows with density for industrial film, a radiograph at density three shows more flaw contrast than the same shot at density one and a half. That is the deep reason behind minimum density rules. More density means more contrast, up to the limit of what your viewer can illuminate. Film makers boil contrast down to one number, the average gradient, the slope measured between two stated densities, commonly two and four. Speed is the other headline number, and on the curve it is simply position left or right. A fast film reaches density two with little exposure, so its curve sits to the left. A slow film needs more, so its curve sits to the right. Compare speeds by drawing a horizontal line at density two and reading the exposure where each curve crosses it. If film A needs twice the exposure of film B, film A is half the speed. What buys speed is grain size. Bigger silver halide crystals catch more radiation per grain, but bigger grains also mean a coarser, grainier image and lower definition. That trade is the heart of film classification. Industry standards group film systems into classes by granularity, speed, and contrast, from the special grade and class one films, the finest grained, slowest, highest definition products, through class two, the fine grain general purpose workhorses, to class three, coarser and faster with lower definition. The class system exists so a code or customer can simply say use class two or finer, and every manufacturer's product line maps onto the requirement. So how do you choose film for a job? Ask four questions. First, what sensitivity does the specification demand? If the contract requires the finest image quality, lean toward the slowest, finest grain film the schedule can stand. Second, what energy are you using? At high energies like cobalt sixty, the subject contrast of the part is inherently low, and a finer, higher gradient film helps claw some of it back. Third, what does production require? A pipeline crew shooting hundreds of welds with a strong source cannot wait on a special grade film. A class two film that meets the required quality level is the professional choice, not the slowest film in the catalog. Fourth, what thickness range has to land on one film? A part with large thickness variation may need a latitude approach, a lower contrast film exposed toward the top of the density window, or two films of different speed loaded in the same cassette so that each thickness lands at a readable density somewhere. Film selection is engineering. Match the curve to the job, prove the result with the required image quality indicator, and write the choice into the technique so the next crew shoots it the same way.
imagePrompt: A film interpreter in a darkened reading room compares two radiographs side by side on a high-intensity illuminator, a densitometer and several boxes of industrial X-ray film stacked on the bench beside him.

sections.heading: The Characteristic (H&D) Curve
sections.body:
A film's **characteristic curve** - the **Hurter and Driffield (H&D) curve** - is its complete personnel file: it plots the **density** produced (vertical axis) against the **logarithm of relative exposure** (horizontal axis) for stated processing.

The log axis matters. Film responds to exposure *ratios*, so equal steps along the log axis are equal multiplications of exposure - **0.3 log units = a factor of 2**. *Relative* exposure is used because absolute units would tie the curve to one kV, distance, and machine; ratios transfer to any setup with the same radiation quality.

Why you care: the curve is the tool behind every density correction. If a radiograph lands at density 1.5 and the code wants 2.0 minimum, the curve tells you exactly how much more exposure buys the difference - read the log-exposure gap between the two densities and convert it to a multiplier. On the straight-line region, **doubling the exposure typically raises density roughly one step up the curve**; the curve replaces that rule of thumb with the precise factor for your film.

sections.caption: The characteristic curve of an industrial film: base-plus-fog, the low-contrast toe, a working region that steepens as density rises, and a shoulder beyond the useful range.

sections.heading: Reading the Regions
sections.body:
Walk the curve left to right:

- **Base + fog.** The curve starts not at zero but at the density of totally unexposed, processed film - the base tint plus the few grains that develop anyway. Fresh film, properly stored and processed, sits around **0.2-0.3**; codes commonly cap base-plus-fog near 0.3 because everything above it is wasted density.
- **Toe.** Density climbs slowly; large exposure differences produce small density differences. Contrast is poor here - which is precisely why codes impose **minimum density** floors. A radiograph living in the toe hides flaws.
- **Working (straight-line) region.** The curve steepens, and for industrial X-ray film it *keeps* steepening as density rises through the whole useful range - unlike pictorial film, there is no flat 'correct exposure' plateau.
- **Shoulder.** Only at very high densities, beyond what a standard illuminator can penetrate, does the curve roll over. For practical purposes the ceiling is set by your viewing equipment, not the film.

sections.heading: Gradient = Film Contrast
sections.body:
The **slope** of the characteristic curve at any density is the **gradient (G)**, and gradient *is* film contrast: it converts a given difference in exposure through the part - a thin slag line, one percent less metal under a flaw - into a density difference your eye can see. A gradient of 4 means a small log-exposure difference is amplified fourfold into density difference.

Because industrial film's gradient **increases with density**, the same weld shot to density 3.0 shows visibly more flaw contrast than at density 1.5. That is the physics behind code minimums: **more density = more contrast**, up to the limit your illuminator can punch through. Manufacturers summarize contrast as the **average gradient**, the slope of the straight line connecting two points on the curve - commonly between densities **2.0 and 4.0**.

The practical habit: expose toward the **upper half of the allowed density window** whenever schedule and viewing equipment permit. Film density 2.0 is a floor to stay above, not a target to sit on.

sections.heading: Film Speed and the Class System
sections.body:
**Speed** is position along the log-exposure axis: a fast film reaches density 2.0 with little exposure (curve to the **left**), a slow film needs more (curve to the **right**). Compare two films by drawing a horizontal line at a reference density - usually 2.0 - and reading the exposure ratio between the crossings. If film A needs twice the exposure of film B, A is half B's speed.

Speed is bought with **grain size**: larger silver halide crystals absorb more radiation per grain and develop to larger silver masses, but they render a coarser, grainier image with lower definition. Hence the universal trade: **finer grain = slower = sharper; coarser grain = faster = grainier.**

Industry film-system standards (ASTM E1815-style classification) group products into **classes by granularity, speed, and contrast** - a *special* grade and Class I at the fine-slow-sharp end, Class II as the general-purpose middle, Class III at the fast-coarse end. The class system lets a code or purchase order simply say 'Class II or finer' and have it mean the same thing across every manufacturer's catalog.

sections.caption: Two films of the same contrast family: the faster film's curve sits to the left, and the horizontal cut at density 2.0 reads the speed difference as an exposure ratio.
Film classes at a glance (generic ASTM E1815-style system).
rows: Finest grain, highest definition and contrast
Critical aerospace work, high-energy shots, finest sensitivity
General weld and casting radiography - the workhorse
Production work where speed governs and quality level allows
Special low-output situations; reciprocity cautions apply

sections.title: Choosing film: the four questions
sections.body:
1. **What sensitivity does the spec demand?** Tighter IQI requirements push toward finer, slower film.
2. **What energy?** High-energy work (Co-60, linacs) has low subject contrast - a higher-gradient, finer film claws some back.
3. **What does production need?** Pick the *fastest* film that still meets the required quality level - not the finest film in the catalog.
4. **What thickness range must land on one film?** Big variation calls for a latitude approach or a two-film load.

sections.heading: Matching Film to the Job
sections.body:
Work three quick cases:

- **Thin turbine-blade weld, tight sensitivity spec, lab setting.** Time is cheap, quality is everything: a **special or Class I** film delivers the finest grain and highest gradient, and the long exposure is acceptable.
- **Pipeline girth welds, hundreds per shift, mid-strength Ir-192.** A **Class II** film that demonstrably meets the required IQI sensitivity is the professional choice; a slower film would blow the schedule without being required by the quality level.
- **Stepped casting, thickness varying 2:1 across the shot.** One film cannot hold every section inside the density window at high contrast. Use a **latitude technique** - or better, a **two-film load**: a faster and a slower film in the same cassette, exposed together. The faster film reaches readable density behind the thick sections; the slower film keeps the thin sections from blowing past the ceiling. Each section is then read on whichever film holds it inside the window.

Whatever you select, the proof is the **image quality indicator** on the finished radiograph - and the film choice gets written into the technique sheet so the next crew duplicates it.

figures.title: Film speed comparison and class trade-offs

interactive.title: Pick the Film for the Job
interactive.intro: Classify each job by the film family a Level II would reach for. Assume every choice must still prove out with the required IQI.

items.label: Turbine-blade weld with the tightest IQI sensitivity in the contract
items.why: When the quality requirement governs and time does not, the finest-grain, highest-gradient film gives the definition and contrast the spec demands.

items.label: High-volume pipeline girth welds with a strong Ir-192 source
items.why: Production economics rule: a Class II film routinely meets typical weld quality levels at a fraction of the exposure time of a special-grade film.

items.label: Co-60 shot where the part's subject contrast is inherently low
items.why: High-energy radiation flattens subject contrast; a finer, higher-gradient film amplifies the small exposure differences that remain.

items.label: Low-output portable X-ray set straining to reach density in the field
items.why: When equipment output is the bottleneck, a faster film cuts exposure to something the machine and schedule can deliver.

items.label: Laboratory failure-analysis radiograph where exposure time is no object
items.why: With no schedule pressure, take the free image quality: the slowest, finest film maximizes definition and contrast.

items.label: Outage window that allows only a short beam-on time for each shot
items.why: A hard time limit is a speed requirement; choose the fastest class that still proves the required IQI sensitivity.

items.label: In a two-film load of a stepped part, the film that must image the thickest section
items.why: Less radiation penetrates the thick section, so the faster film is the one that reaches readable density there while the slower film covers the thin sections.

items.label: Fine crack detection near the acceptance limit on an aerospace weld
items.why: Tight cracks produce tiny exposure differences; fine grain and high gradient give the sharpness and contrast needed to render them visible.

checkQuestions.question: On a characteristic (H&D) curve, what is plotted against what?
options: Film density versus the logarithm of relative exposure
Film density versus kilovoltage
Exposure time versus source activity
Film density versus material thickness
checkQuestions.explanation: The H&D curve plots density against log relative exposure. The log axis is used because film responds to exposure ratios - 0.3 log units is a factor of two in exposure.

checkQuestions.question: Unexposed, properly processed fresh film typically shows a density of about 0.2-0.3. This residual density is called:
options: The toe density
Base plus fog
The average gradient
Reciprocity density
checkQuestions.explanation: Base-plus-fog is the base tint plus the few grains that develop without exposure. Codes commonly limit it to about 0.3 because it adds density without adding image information.

checkQuestions.question: Why do codes set a minimum density (typically around 2.0) for radiographs?
options: Lower densities fade with time in storage
Film gradient - and therefore flaw contrast - is poor at low density and increases with density
Densitometers cannot read below density 2.0
Lower densities indicate the film was overexposed
checkQuestions.explanation: Industrial film's gradient rises with density, so a radiograph in the toe region converts thickness differences into much smaller density differences. The minimum keeps the image in a region of useful contrast.

checkQuestions.question: Film X reaches density 2.0 with 10 mA·min; film Y needs 20 mA·min under identical conditions. Which statement is correct?
options: Film Y is twice as fast as film X
Film X is twice as fast, and its curve sits to the left of film Y's
Film X is twice as fast, and its curve sits to the right of film Y's
The films have equal speed but different contrast
checkQuestions.explanation: Needing half the exposure to reach the reference density makes film X twice the speed, and faster films' curves sit toward the low-exposure (left) end of the log-exposure axis.

checkQuestions.question: A contract requires radiographing thick steel with Co-60 and flags that subject contrast will be low. The best film strategy is:
options: The fastest Class III film to shorten exposure
A fluorescent-screen film for maximum speed
A finer-grain, higher-gradient film to recover contrast, accepting the longer exposure
Any film, since film choice cannot affect contrast
checkQuestions.explanation: At high energy the part itself produces small exposure differences, so the film's gradient must amplify them; finer, higher-gradient film classes are the standard answer to low subject contrast.

references: CP-105-2024 RT Topical Outline, Radiography Technique 3.4-3.6
Film-system classification standards such as ASTM E1815 (general)
Codes such as ASME BPVC Section V, Article 2 (density limits, general practice)
Film manufacturer characteristic-curve data sheets (generic examples)
